macro_segments.title: Hymenal bleeding attribution when sex occurs without bleeding on first encounter but with bleeding on second encounter

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina versus Rav Assi on whether second bleeding is menstrual or hymenal
Shmuel's statement that multiple acts of sex with a virgin can occur without bleeding
Distinction that Shmuel's ability is unique and not applicable to ordinary people

macro_segments.title: Attribution period for hymenal bleeding based on girl's age and maturity status (ketanah, naarah, bogeret)

micro_segments.title: Rav's ruling on bogeret (post-puberty woman) who has not yet had her period versus who has had her period
Rebbi's leniency: four acts of sex spread over twelve months attributed as hymenal bleeding
Gemara's analysis of which age categories Rebbi's ruling spans (ketanah, naarah, bogeret)
Rav's answer on transitioning from ketanah to naarah: multiple pre-puberty acts count as one toward the four-night limit
Rebbi's intended scenario clarified: one act as ketanah, three acts as naarah over twelve-month period

macro_segments.title: Digression on kashrering olive presses and wine vats: definition of onah (period of time) in terms of hymenal bleeding

micro_segments.title: Mishna on cleaning טמא (tamei) wine vats and olive presses through rinsing, drying, or aging
Twelve-month period for absorption of tamei liquids versus alternative methods of kashering
Definition of onah: day and night versus half-day and half-night depending on season
Reconciling Beis Hillel's 'night and half day' with standard onah of twelve hours

macro_segments.title: Major shift in halacha: transition from attributing hymenal bleeding to treating all bleeding as niddah bleeding requiring immediate separation

micro_segments.title: Rav and Shmuel's ruling: one act of sex followed by separation (boel biat mitzvah u'poresh)
Ulla's statement: this perk (tinukim) yields no practical law; conclusion is always boel biat mitzvah u'poresh

macro_segments.title: Challenges and justifications for treating hymenal bleeding as niddah blood despite biological difference

micro_segments.title: Rav Abba's question: if hymenal bleeding is treated as niddah, how can the couple complete the act on wedding night?

macro_segments.title: Challenges and justifications for treating hymenal bleeding as niddah blood despite biological difference (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara's resolution: a religiously sensitive person (ba'al nefesh) will naturally withdraw if bleeding begins
Lecturer's explanation: stringency likely stems from broader movement toward stringency in all blood issues, not logically justified
Ramban's radical interpretation (rejected): if hymenal blood treated as niddah, then all wound blood should be treated as niddah
Other rishonim's response: stringency applies only to hymenal blood because it occurs once and unavoidably
Possible explanation: fear of mixing hymenal blood with menstrual or excitement-induced blood (dam chimmud)
Observation: stringencies in hymenal bleeding post-date concerns about tumah and introduce novel stringencies absent in Temple period

macro_segments.title: Major shift in halacha: transition from attributing hymenal bleeding to treating all bleeding as niddah bleeding requiring immediate separation (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's challenge: Rebbi gave four nights even over one year, contradicting one-act separation rule
Gemara's resolution: Rav and Shmuel ruled against the Mishnah based on later rabbinical consensus (rabboteinu chazru v'nimnu)

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Chanina vs. Rav Assi dispute
Shmuel's statement on multiple acts without bleeding
Beis Shammai vs. Beis Hillel on attribution period
Boel biat mitzvah u'poresh (one act then separate)
Rabboteinu chazru v'nimnu (rabbis reconvened and voted)
